title: Hyperkeratosis -- Differential Diagnosis
content: The overall differential diagnosis for hyperkeratosis is extremely broad and includes benign dermatological diseases as well as malignant ones. Overlapping clinical features make the diagnosis and workup challenging. A correct anamnesis, patient examination, and the appropriate use of skin biopsy are useful for the differential diagnosis. Differential diagnosis of hyperkeratosis: Callus and Corns Keratosis plantare Chronic folliculitis Atopic dermatitis Psoriasis and psoriasiform dermatitis Lichen planus and lichenoid dermatitis Keratosis pilaris Ichthyoses Seborrheic keratosis Actinic keratosis Keratoacanthoma Paraneoplastic syndromes Squamous cell carcinoma Basal cell carcinoma

title: Pustular Psoriasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Management of pustular psoriasis requires an interprofessional team approach, including providers from primary care, dermatology, and infectious disease. A detailed history and complete physical examination are critical in diagnosing cases of pustular psoriasis. A dermatologist can help in the initial diagnostic workup and to assess for complications. Depending on the surface area involved, admission to the hospital may be required (e.g., von Zumbusch subtype). In equivocal cases, a biopsy can be performed, as the differential diagnosis for pustulosis is rather vast. Treatment is targeted to prevent relapses and shorten the duration of flares.

title: Porokeratosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
content: Porokeratosis is often found in sun-exposed areas and is noted in the fifth decade of life. Interestingly, several different clinical variants of porokeratosis exist, but the exact etiology and pathology of these lesions remain unclear. Of all the clinical variants, linear and giant porokeratosis have the worst prognosis, as they are most likely to undergo malignant transformation. On the other hand, malignant transformation is rare in disseminated superficial actinic porokeratosis. There may be a multifactorial cause of Porokeratosis with possible etiologies, including UV light, trauma, genetics, infectious agents, and immunosuppression.

This linkage underscores that the presence of a metastatic lesion signals an underlying, site‑unspecified malignant neoplasm, which may be noted in the context of trauma‑related comorbidities and serves as the main diagnostic finding for the patient.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head is a broad category of cancer spread that encompasses more specific manifestations such as metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head. The latter represents a distinct subset of head metastases, focusing on involvement of the regional lymphatic structures. Because metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head reflects disease that has spread from a primary site, it is classified under secondary malignancies, the overarching term for cancers that arise as metastases rather than as primary tumors. Recognizing that lymph node involvement in the head is both a specific form of head metastasis and a type of secondary malignancy helps clinicians accurately stage disease, guide treatment planning, and communicate prognosis.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2712
content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, referred to in Arabic as نقائل إلى الرقبة, represents a secondary malignancy in which cancer cells have spread from a primary site to the cervical lymph nodes. Recognizing this condition as a secondary malignancy guides clinicians in staging, prognosis, and therapeutic decisions, emphasizing the importance of identifying metastatic involvement of neck lymph nodes in patient care.
